## Guide to Agile Development and Community Participation on Discord Welcome to our project’s Discord server! We're excited to have you join us as we build this Magic: The Gathering (MTG) Draft Overlay, Deck Builder, and Cube Assistant . We use Agile Development to keep the project moving efficiently, and your participation is key to our success. This guide explains how we use Agile methods to structure the work and how you can get involved at every stage. ### What is Agile Development? Agile Development is a flexible, iterative approach to software development. Instead of planning everything upfront, we break the project into smaller chunks called sprints (1-2 weeks long). Each sprint focuses on specific features, and at the end of each sprint, we release a functional piece of the project for feedback and testing. Here’s how the Agile process works in our project: 1. Backlog : A prioritized list of tasks and features we need to develop. 2. Sprint Planning : At the beginning of each sprint, we pick tasks from the backlog to work on. 3. Development : We code, build, and test features. 4. Community Testing : At the end of each sprint, we release the feature for community testing and feedback. 5. Review and Adjust : Based on the feedback, we make improvements, plan for the next sprint, and repeat the process. ### How We Use Agile in the Project #### 1. The Backlog The backlog contains all the features, improvements, and bugs that need to be worked on. We keep this list updated on our GitHub project board , which is linked in the #project-management channel. The community can view the backlog, suggest new features, and upvote the ones they think are most important. - Where to Find It : #backlog-updates and #project-management channels. - How You Can Contribute : Suggest new features, report bugs, or upvote existing tasks. Your input helps us prioritize what to work on next. #### 2. Sprint Planning At the beginning of each sprint, we choose a few key tasks to work on. These tasks are usually taken from the top-priority items in the backlog. Each sprint has clear goals, and by the end of the sprint, we aim to have a working feature or improvement. - Where to Find It : #sprint-planning channel. - How You Can Contribute : Suggest tasks for the next sprint or volunteer to help work on a specific feature if you have coding skills or can help test. #### 3. Development During the sprint, we build the selected features and share progress updates regularly. We might need help with feedback on design, functionality, or even with writing documentation. - Where to Find It : #development and #coding-help channels. - How You Can Contribute : If you're a developer, feel free to take on a coding task. If you're not a coder, you can help by reviewing the features as they're being built. #### 4. Community Testing Once a sprint is complete, we need help testing the new features. Testing ensures the features work as expected and identifies bugs or improvements. After testing, we’ll collect feedback and adjust the feature in the next sprint. - Where to Find It : #beta-testing and #feature-feedback channels. - How You Can Contribute : Test the new features by trying them out in your browser or app. Share your feedback on what works, what doesn’t, and any suggestions you have for improvement. #### 5. Review and Adjust At the end of each sprint, we review the feedback and decide what changes to make. This step is crucial for ensuring we’re building the right features and fixing issues early. We then plan the next sprint, keeping the project moving forward in small, manageable steps. - Where to Find It : #sprint-retrospective channel. - How You Can Contribute : Participate in retrospective discussions. Tell us what went well, what could be improved, and any ideas for the next sprint. --- ### How You Can Get Involved #### 1. Join a Sprint If you want to be more involved in the development process, consider joining a sprint. You don’t have to be a coder to participate—there are plenty of ways to help: - Test features : Try out new functionality and report bugs. - Give feedback : Share ideas on how the app should work or look. - Create content : Help with writing tutorials, guides, or promotional materials. - Design : If you have graphic design skills, help us improve the app’s UI/UX. #### 2. Report Issues and Suggest Features Even if you're not working on the code, you can still help by reporting issues you find when testing the app. If you have ideas for new features or improvements, let us know! We have a #suggestions channel specifically for this purpose. #### 3. Help Moderate or Organize We’re always looking for community moderators to help keep the Discord channels organized and friendly. If you’re interested in a more leadership-oriented role, consider helping with sprint planning or backlog management. --- ### Our Discord Channels for Agile Development To keep everything organized, we have several channels dedicated to different parts of the project. Here’s a breakdown of where to find everything: - #announcements : Updates on new features, sprints, and community events. - #backlog-updates : The latest updates on what’s in the backlog and what we’re working on. - #sprint-planning : Discussions about what tasks are in the next sprint. - #development : Ongoing conversations about building features and writing code. - #coding-help : A place for developers to ask questions and share knowledge. - #beta-testing : When new features are ready, this is where you can find them and help test. - #feature-feedback : After testing, share your feedback here—what works, what needs improvement. - #sprint-retrospective : At the end of each sprint, we reflect on what went well and what can be improved. - #suggestions : Your ideas and feature requests go here. - #general : For anything else that doesn’t fit into the above categories. --- ### Why Community Participation Matters Our goal is to build an app that is not only functional but also fun and useful for the Magic: The Gathering community. By using Agile Development and working in sprints, we can get feedback quickly, adjust features based on your needs, and create something that evolves with the community. Your participation helps shape the project, making it better for everyone involved. Whether you’re a developer, designer, tester, or just passionate about Magic, your contributions make a difference. --- ### Conclusion Thank you for being part of this journey! By following Agile methods, we can make steady progress, continuously improve the app, and ensure that the features we’re building are exactly what the community needs. Whether you're here to code, test, or just offer feedback, there’s a role for everyone. Let’s get things done, one sprint at a time! If you have any questions, feel free to ask in the #general or #help channels. Let’s build something great together!

### Blog Post: Using Narrative Design to Empower Magic: The Gathering Players with Our Draft and Gameplay Tools In Magic: The Gathering (MTG) , every deck tells a story. It could be the saga of an aggro deck rushing to victory or the slow, methodical control player carefully weaving a tapestry of inevitability. As we develop our MTG Draft Overlay, Deck Recommendation Tool, and Gameplay Helper , we are using narrative design principles to do more than just crunch numbers. Our goal is to craft a tool that helps players navigate their decisions and, more importantly, empowers them to tell the story of their own deck . ### Narrative Design and Recommendation Engines When we think of recommendation engines—whether for a draft, deck building, or in-game plays—it's easy to fall into the trap of reducing everything to raw statistics. However, in MTG, it’s not just about data; it’s about the story that unfolds with each card draw, each creature played, and each spell cast. Narrative design comes into play by ensuring that our tools don’t simply hand players the “best” decisions in a vacuum but instead guide them to decisions that align with the story they want to tell with their deck . Here’s how we approach this: #### 1. Draft Navigation: Guiding a Narrative of Possibilities Every draft tells a story in real-time. As the player navigates through each pack, they are making choices that build toward the final deck—a narrative that starts with a single card pick and unfolds into an archetype or color combination. We want to make sure our draft overlay helps the player feel like they are discovering and building their narrative rather than being passively guided by an algorithm. - Telling a Story with Draft Picks : Instead of simply saying, "This card has the highest win rate," we use language that reflects the journey the player is on. For example, if a player starts picking aggressive creatures, our tool might say, “You’re on a path to building a quick, overwhelming force. Keep an eye out for cheap, powerful removal spells to support your attack.” - Recognizing Archetypes as Storylines : MTG archetypes are more than just mechanical combinations; they represent different narratives. A sacrifice deck, for example, tells a story of making short-term losses for long-term gains. Our overlay could recognize when a player starts drafting key sacrifice enablers and shift its recommendations toward supporting that evolving story . This approach provides a sense of continuity that isn’t just about maximizing card value, but about how those cards fit into the larger deck narrative . #### 2. Deck Building: Empowering the Player as the Author Once a draft is over or when a player is refining a deck, the deck-building process is their chance to solidify the story their deck will tell. Our recommendation tool doesn’t just suggest cards based on raw data but helps players craft a narrative around their choices. - Synergy as a Narrative Arc : In many ways, synergy is the glue that holds a deck’s story together. It’s the reason why certain cards feel right together, even beyond their individual strengths. When we recommend cards based on synergy, we aim to explain that synergy in terms of the story the player is crafting. For example, if the player is building a deck around +1/+1 counters, the tool might suggest, “This card will allow your creatures to grow, reflecting your deck’s theme of steady progression and resilience.” - Playstyle and Personal Narrative : A player’s personal playstyle is key to the story they tell with their deck. Are they a control player, laying traps and patiently outlasting their opponent? Or are they an aggressive player, always on the offensive? Our deck-building tool doesn’t just recommend the mathematically best cards—it also takes into account how a player prefers to win games , so that the final product is something that feels natural and enjoyable for them to play. #### 3. Gameplay Assistance: Supporting the Player’s Tactical Narrative During gameplay, the player is actively telling their deck’s story in real-time. Each play they make is a new chapter in that narrative. Our gameplay helper will act as an adviser , but not a narrator—it’s important that the player feels agency in their decisions. - Real-Time Storytelling : The gameplay feedback system will use narrative-driven advice . Instead of saying, “This is a bad play,” we might frame it as, “Are you sure this supports your overall plan? Saving this removal for a key threat might help your long-term survival.” This way, the player is gently guided while still remaining the author of their own decisions. - Empowering the Player's Story : The system can recognize patterns that align with the player's deck’s theme. For example, if a player is using a control deck that relies on board wipes and careful timing, the helper might suggest, “It’s tempting to deal with this now, but waiting for them to overextend could fit your strategy better.” This kind of messaging respects the player’s intentionality and empowers them to feel that they are crafting the game’s outcome—not just following instructions. ### Empowering the Player’s Narrative with Data At its core, Magic is a game of choices, but the complexity of the game can sometimes make those choices overwhelming. By integrating narrative design principles , we’re ensuring that our recommendation engine doesn’t just spit out cold, hard data—it helps the player see how those numbers fit into the story of their deck . #### Data as Context, Not Command We don’t want our users to feel as though they are simply following instructions from a machine. By using data to provide context rather than commands , we help players see the why behind their decisions. For example, instead of saying “This card has a high win rate, pick it,” we provide more depth: “This card has a high win rate in control decks because it helps you stabilize against aggressive opponents, which fits with the defensive tools you’ve already drafted.” #### Using Data to Encourage Exploration Magic thrives on creativity, and part of our goal is to encourage players to explore new strategies and tell new stories with their decks. The app might suggest cards that deviate from the meta, allowing players to try something fresh. “This card doesn’t see as much play, but it works well with the synergy you’re building—this could be the linchpin of a unique strategy!” ### The Player as the Protagonist At the heart of our app is the idea that the player is the protagonist . Everything we design—whether it’s draft suggestions, deck recommendations, or gameplay advice—aims to keep the player in the driver’s seat of their own narrative. We’re not telling the player what to do; we’re offering guidance to help them craft the story they want to tell with their deck. #### Respecting Player Agency One of the most important narrative principles is to always respect the agency of the protagonist. In the same way, we ensure that our app respects the player’s agency . At no point do we want to remove the player's feeling of control or make them feel like they’re playing on autopilot. Instead, our goal is to provide them with enough information, options, and advice to make decisions that they feel ownership over. #### The Player’s Story Beyond the Game Each deck tells a story within the game, but for many Magic players, the story doesn’t end when the match is over. Whether it’s a memorable draft, a great combo, or a clever play, these moments become part of the player’s personal Magic journey. By helping players refine their draft and deck-building decisions, our app contributes to the larger story of their experience with Magic, giving them the tools to remember and celebrate their own successes. ### Conclusion: Narrative Design for a Personalized Magic Experience Building a tool that incorporates narrative design principles allows us to create something far more than just a recommendation engine. We’re building a narrative assistant that helps each player tell the best possible version of their Magic story—whether that’s through drafting synergistic cards, building decks that reflect their personal playstyle, or making in-game decisions that bring their strategic vision to life. Ultimately, Magic: The Gathering is a game of stories. Whether you’re a seasoned player or new to the game, our tools are designed to help you make choices that reflect your unique deck-building and gameplay narrative, turning data into decisions and decisions into the epic stories of your own Magic journey .
Explain how we are telling stories about things like power levels and synergies and the parameters of a format.

### Telling Stories Through Power Levels, Synergies, and Format Parameters in Magic: The Gathering When we talk about Magic: The Gathering (MTG) , we aren't just talking about cards, decks, and numbers. We're talking about stories —the narratives that unfold as players build decks, draft cards, and engage in combat. Each decision, from choosing the most synergistic card in a draft to understanding the power level of a deck, contributes to a larger narrative. As we develop our MTG Draft Overlay, Deck Recommendation Tool, and Gameplay Helper , we're using these concepts— power levels , synergies , and format parameters —to tell stories that help players better understand the game and craft their own strategic journeys. ### 1. Power Levels: The Strength of the Protagonist Power levels are a critical part of understanding any deck or card. But they’re more than just a number; they’re a reflection of the role a card plays in the story of the deck . A card with a high power level might be a deck’s hero—a crucial piece that turns the tide in a game, while lower power cards might be the supporting cast, helping to execute the overall strategy. #### Power Levels as Character Strengths In our app, power levels are more than just metrics for optimization. They are story elements that reflect how a card functions within the broader narrative of a deck. For example: - High-Power Cards : These are the deck’s champions, the big finishers, or key combo pieces that embody the deck's winning strategy. When our tool identifies high-power cards, we don't just say, “This is strong”; we might say, “This card is the anchor of your deck’s late-game strategy. It’s your trump card when you need to take control of the game.” - Low-Power Cards : Low-power cards might not be flashy, but they have their role in the narrative, providing consistency or enabling synergies. For instance, our tool might frame a low-power card as, “This card fills a crucial gap in your early game, setting the stage for your stronger plays to follow.” By framing power levels in this narrative context , we help the player understand how individual cards contribute to the overall arc of their deck’s story, making the drafting or deck-building process more engaging and meaningful. ### 2. Synergies: The Relationships in the Narrative In MTG, synergy refers to how well certain cards work together—how the effects of one card enhance or enable the effects of another. But synergies aren’t just mechanical; they are the interwoven relationships that create a cohesive deck. A deck’s synergy tells the story of how it functions, how each piece supports the others, and how, together, they form a strategy that’s greater than the sum of its parts. #### Synergies as Story Arcs In the same way that characters in a novel have relationships and interconnected roles, so do the cards in a deck. When we talk about synergies in our tool, we emphasize how these interactions create a narrative arc within the deck: - Synergy as Momentum : In a synergistic deck, each card contributes to the momentum of the strategy. For example, a +1/+1 counter deck might tell the story of creatures growing and evolving over the course of the game, getting stronger with each turn. Our recommendation engine could say something like, “This card helps fuel your growing army, strengthening your creatures over time and building your board presence.” - Synergy as Theme : Some synergies are thematic—like tribal decks, where the cards share a type (such as Zombies or Elves) and build on each other’s abilities. In these cases, we might describe the synergy in terms of an overarching theme: “You’re building a Zombie horde. This card will rally your undead forces, increasing their strength as the game progresses.” By presenting synergies as narrative relationships , we help players not only recognize the importance of card interactions but also understand how their choices create a cohesive and compelling deck that tells a unique story in every game. ### 3. Format Parameters: The Setting of the Story The format in which a deck is built and played—whether it's Draft, Standard, Commander, or Cube—acts like the setting of a story . The format provides the rules, boundaries, and context within which the narrative unfolds. Just as the setting of a novel shapes the actions of its characters, the parameters of a format shape the way players build their decks and strategize. #### Format as the World-Building Each format has its own set of rules, card pool, and dynamics, much like how different genres in fiction have different tropes and conventions. In our app, we consider the format’s parameters when guiding players, helping them understand how these rules define the types of stories they can tell with their deck: - Draft Format : In Draft, the format’s constraints (random card pools, limited resources) tell a story of adaptation and discovery . Our draft overlay could emphasize this, saying something like, “You’re in a draft where resources are scarce—this card might not seem powerful on its own, but it could be the piece you need to unlock a key synergy later.” - Commander Format : Commander, with its singleton rules and focus on 100-card decks, tells stories of long-term planning and intricate interactions . Our tool might highlight this by saying, “This card’s uniqueness in your Commander deck gives you an unexpected advantage—use it to surprise opponents who may not see it coming.” - Cube Format : Cube drafting often tells stories of mastery and curation , where every card has been hand-picked for balance and excitement. We might guide players through cube drafts with advice like, “This card is a hidden gem in this well-curated environment—its synergy with other high-powered cards in the cube can give you an edge.” By treating each format as a world with its own rules and context, we help players understand the setting they’re working within, guiding them to make decisions that fit the unique challenges and opportunities of that format. ### Empowering Players to Be Storytellers At the heart of our app’s design is the belief that players are the authors of their own stories. The draft overlay, deck-building tools, and gameplay assistant we are developing are not there to dictate or overrule the player’s choices. Instead, they act as guides , offering insights and suggestions that allow the player to craft a more engaging, cohesive, and personal narrative . #### Turning Strategy into Storytelling When we talk about power levels, synergies, or formats, we’re not just talking about mechanics—we’re helping the player tell a story with their deck. Our app will encourage players to explore these elements creatively, seeing each choice as a part of a broader narrative rather than a simple optimization problem. For instance: - Instead of saying, “This card has the best stats, pick it,” we might say, “This card represents the turning point in your deck’s strategy, where you shift from defense to offense.” - When offering synergy-based recommendations, we might frame the decision as part of a narrative arc , guiding the player through how their choices lead to a satisfying climax in gameplay. In doing so, we shift the focus from pure data to empowerment —helping players understand not only the strategic implications of their decisions but also how those decisions contribute to the unfolding story of their deck . ### Conclusion: Crafting a Narrative of Strategy Building a recommendation engine, draft overlay, or gameplay assistant for MTG isn’t just about delivering the most optimized choices—it’s about creating a narrative framework that helps players understand the why behind those choices. Power levels tell the story of a deck’s key characters. Synergies reveal the interconnected relationships that drive a deck forward. Format parameters define the setting, shaping the challenges and opportunities players face. By thinking about these elements through the lens of narrative design , we’re not only building tools that improve gameplay, but also tools that empower players to become the storytellers of their own MTG journey . Every deck, every game, every card choice is an opportunity to craft a unique narrative, and our app is here to help make that narrative both compelling and strategic.

### 6. ALSA vs. GIH Win-Rate Insights The ALSA (Average Last Seen At) vs. GIH (Games in Hand) win-rate analysis is an important part of helping players make informed draft choices. It provides valuable insight into whether a card is undervalued or overvalued in the draft pool, allowing players to make strategic decisions based on community trends. #### Key Components: - Efficient Data Retrieval - Fetch ALSA and GIH win-rate data from a reliable API (such as 17lands) for each card in the draft pool, ensuring that the data is up-to-date and relevant to the current meta. - Next Step : Integrate the data fetching logic into the draft overlay, ensuring that it retrieves data for every card in the current pack. Code Snippet Example : const fetchALSAandGIH = async (cardName) => { const response = await fetch( https://api.17lands.com/card_ratings ); const data = await response.json(); return data.find(card => card.name === cardName); }; const getCardAnalysis = async (cardName) => { const cardData = await fetchALSAandGIH(cardName); return analyzeCardPick(cardData); }; - Comparative Analysis Between ALSA and GIH - Use the ALSA vs. GIH data to inform players whether a card is typically picked earlier or later in the draft and how well it performs when played. - Next Step : Implement a more sophisticated comparison logic that takes into account both the card's pick value (ALSA) and its actual game performance (GIH). Tell me more about how our strategy module will be thinking about. Game plans like agro mid-range and control or hidden deck architypes.

### Understanding Game Plans and Archetypes: How Our Strategy Module Analyzes Aggro, Midrange, Control, and Hidden Archetypes In Magic: The Gathering (MTG) , understanding your deck’s game plan is key to developing a cohesive and effective strategy. Broadly, game plans are categorized into Aggro , Midrange , and Control archetypes, with more nuanced strategies and hidden archetypes like Combo , Ramp , or Tempo forming subcategories or hybrid strategies. Our strategy module is designed to analyze these game plans as they develop during drafts or deck-building and guide players on how to optimize their choices to fit a cohesive archetype. Here’s how the strategy module will think about game plans and archetypes: ### 1. Aggro: Speed and Pressure Aggro decks are defined by their aggressive, fast-paced game plan. The strategy focuses on applying pressure early and consistently, often overwhelming opponents before they have a chance to stabilize. In Aggro, tempo and curve are key—players want to maximize their early-game power, deploying threats quickly and forcing their opponents to respond. #### How Our Module Analyzes Aggro: - Early-Pick Signals : If the player starts picking low-cost, aggressive creatures or efficient burn spells early in the draft, the strategy module will begin recommending other fast, low-cost cards to fit the Aggro archetype. It will emphasize creatures with evasion, haste, or abilities that generate incremental damage. - Mana Curve Optimization : In Aggro, the mana curve needs to be low and consistent. The module will provide suggestions to smooth the curve, recommending more 1-3 mana creatures if the deck has too many expensive spells. - Synergy Over Power : In Aggro, synergy can often be more important than raw power. The module will prioritize cards that work together to push through damage quickly—cards that buff other creatures or work with key mechanics like haste, trample, or flying. #### Key Draft Overlay Messages for Aggro: - "You’re drafting a fast aggro deck—prioritize low-cost creatures to overwhelm your opponent early." - "Look for cheap removal spells or combat tricks to keep the pressure on while removing blockers." - "Your deck is curving out well, but you could use another 1-drop to ensure a fast start." ### 2. Midrange: Flexibility and Value Midrange decks aim to stabilize the game during the mid-game (turns 4-6) by deploying resilient creatures and generating incremental card advantage. Midrange decks often adapt to the situation—acting aggressively against slower opponents but shifting into a more defensive role against faster decks. The hallmark of Midrange decks is their value —each card is designed to provide multiple forms of advantage, like card draw, life gain, or board presence. #### How Our Module Analyzes Midrange: - Balanced Curve : In Midrange decks, having a balanced mana curve with threats across different stages of the game is essential. The module will guide players to pick efficient mid-game creatures and removal spells that provide flexibility. - Value Creatures : The module will recommend creatures that provide extra value—like those with ETB (Enter the Battlefield) effects or creatures that can generate card advantage over time. These creatures will ensure the player can keep applying pressure while gaining resources. - Adapting the Strategy : Midrange decks can shift from defense to offense based on the matchup. The module will analyze the deck’s ability to handle both aggressive and control opponents, recommending versatile cards that can serve both roles. #### Key Draft Overlay Messages for Midrange: - "You’re drafting a strong midrange deck—prioritize creatures with value-generating abilities to stabilize the mid-game." - "This removal spell fits well in your midrange strategy, allowing you to control the board and pivot to aggression." - "Your deck has enough early game—focus on solidifying your mid-game with more resilient threats." ### 3. Control: Patience and Dominance Control decks focus on survival until the late game, where they can turn the corner and dominate with powerful finishers. Control players seek to stall the game , using removal, counterspells, and card draw to maintain card advantage while neutralizing threats. The goal is to outlast the opponent by neutralizing all their win conditions and then winning with a single overwhelming finisher. #### How Our Module Analyzes Control: - Early-Pick Control Signals : If a player starts drafting counterspells, board wipes, or card draw spells, the module will identify this as a Control strategy and start recommending more defensive and reactive cards. - Focus on Removal : Control decks need plenty of removal and counterspells to neutralize threats. The module will prioritize cards that deal with multiple threats (e.g., board wipes) or efficient spot removal. - Finishers : The module will recommend high-impact finishers (e.g., planeswalkers or large creatures) that can close out a game once control has been established. It will also suggest cards that provide recursion or inevitability. #### Key Draft Overlay Messages for Control: - "You’re building a control deck—prioritize board wipes and counterspells to maintain control of the game." - "This card draw spell will help you outlast aggressive decks and find your win condition." - "You have enough removal—start looking for a powerful finisher to close out the game." ### 4. Hidden Archetypes: Combo, Ramp, Tempo, etc. In addition to the traditional Aggro, Midrange, and Control archetypes, hidden archetypes like Combo , Ramp , and Tempo offer alternative strategies that don’t fit neatly into one of the primary categories. These archetypes often rely on specific synergies or mechanics to win the game in unexpected ways. #### How Our Module Analyzes Hidden Archetypes: Combo: - Combo decks focus on assembling specific cards that combine to create a win condition, often winning the game in a single turn. Combo decks require precise card selection and deck manipulation (e.g., card draw, scrying) to assemble the combo pieces quickly. - Strategy Module’s Role : The module will detect early picks of key combo pieces and start suggesting cards that support assembling the combo, such as card draw, tutors, and mana ramp. - *Message*: "You’re drafting a combo deck—focus on finding the rest of your combo pieces and support cards." Ramp: - Ramp decks aim to accelerate their mana production to cast large, game-winning threats earlier than normal. Ramp decks typically rely on mana dorks (creatures that tap for mana) or spells that fetch lands from the library. - Strategy Module’s Role : When ramp cards are drafted early, the module will start prioritizing high-cost creatures and spells, as well as additional ramp cards to ensure the player can reach enough mana quickly. - *Message*: "You’re building a ramp deck—prioritize large creatures and more ramp spells to accelerate your game plan." Tempo: - Tempo decks seek to disrupt the opponent’s game plan while developing their own board, often through efficient creatures and spells that temporarily slow down the opponent (e.g., bounce spells or tap effects). - Strategy Module’s Role : The module will suggest efficient creatures and spells that help the player stay ahead of their opponent, focusing on maintaining board presence while limiting the opponent’s ability to respond. - *Message*: "Your deck is taking a tempo approach—focus on cheap creatures and spells that can disrupt your opponent’s game plan while developing your own board." --- ### How the Strategy Module Identifies Hidden Archetypes During Draft - Combo Identification : If the player picks up multiple pieces of a known combo (e.g., "Splinter Twin" and "Pestermite"), the module will detect the combo strategy and begin suggesting support cards. - *Message*: "You’ve drafted key combo pieces—start picking up cards that help you assemble the combo." - Ramp Identification : If the player drafts multiple ramp spells or mana-producing creatures, the module will prioritize recommending high-cost creatures and spells that fit a ramp strategy. - *Message*: "You’ve drafted enough ramp—start looking for game-ending threats to capitalize on your mana advantage." - Tempo Identification : If the player’s early picks include efficient creatures and bounce spells or removal, the module will start suggesting additional tempo-supporting cards like evasive creatures or low-cost interaction. - *Message*: "You’re building a tempo deck—prioritize spells that let you keep the pressure on while disrupting your opponent." --- ### Key Takeaways for Game Plan Analysis - Real-Time Adjustments : The strategy module continuously analyzes the deck and suggests adjustments as the draft progresses. It identifies the deck’s game plan based on early picks and makes recommendations that keep the player on track. - Archetype Support : Whether the player is drafting Aggro, Midrange, Control, or a hidden archetype, the module ensures the player has the tools they need to fully execute their game plan. - Nuanced Guidance : By providing specific advice about how to balance mana curves, prioritize synergy, and adapt to the opponent’s deck, the module helps players optimize their strategy at every step of the draft and gameplay. This approach allows players to develop strong, cohesive decks while gaining deeper insight into how different archetypes function and how to identify hidden archetypes based on the cards they draft.

Tell me more about how the draft navigator will deal With lanes pivoting and splashing considerations

The draft navigator will be designed to guide players through key decision points in a draft, especially when it comes to staying in lanes , pivoting , and splashing . These are crucial aspects of draft strategy, as they involve recognizing when to commit to an archetype, when to change direction, and when to stretch into a third color for additional power or synergy. ### 1. Staying in Lanes In Magic drafts, the concept of "staying in your lane" refers to committing to a particular color or archetype once you've received strong signals that it’s open (i.e., underdrafted by other players). The draft navigator will help players identify their lane and stick to it when it's clearly the best option. #### How the Navigator Helps Players Stay in Lanes: - Signal Reading : The navigator will monitor the cards passed to the player and help them recognize when a particular color or archetype is underdrafted. For example, if the player sees late-pick high-quality red cards, the navigator will highlight that red is likely open, suggesting that the player stick to this color. Prompt Example : - *"You’re receiving late red cards—this suggests red is open. Consider sticking to red and drafting more red-based cards in the next few picks."* - Archetype Tracking : As the draft progresses, the navigator will help the player stay on track by analyzing their picks and recommending cards that fit their established color pair or archetype. This ensures that the player doesn’t accidentally drift into a weaker lane. Prompt Example : - *"You’ve already picked several solid removal spells and creatures in black—prioritize black cards to stay on track with your current plan."* - Commitment Advice : The navigator will provide guidance on when to fully commit to a lane versus when to stay open. If the player has received consistent signals that a color is open (such as multiple strong cards in one color across several packs), the navigator will suggest solidifying that color choice. Prompt Example : - *"At this stage, you’ve received strong signals for blue. It’s safe to commit to blue as a primary color."* ### 2. Pivoting Pivoting is the process of switching to a different color or archetype during a draft, often because your current color isn’t open or you’re receiving stronger signals for another color. Pivoting is a critical skill in drafts, as staying flexible allows you to capitalize on underdrafted strategies. #### How the Navigator Facilitates Pivoting: - Signal-Based Pivot Alerts : The draft navigator will monitor the cards passed to the player, alerting them when stronger signals emerge for a different color or archetype. For example, if the player starts seeing high-quality white cards after picking mostly green in the first few picks, the navigator might suggest a pivot into white-green or even white-blue if that’s stronger. Prompt Example : - *"You’ve seen strong white cards in the last two packs. Consider pivoting to white to take advantage of an underdrafted color."* - Late-Pick Pivoting Advice : If the player is in the middle of a draft and their current strategy isn’t fully supported (e.g., they’re not getting the payoff cards they need for their archetype), the navigator will suggest a pivot into a more open lane. Prompt Example : - *"You’re not receiving enough synergy pieces for your aggressive black-red deck. Consider pivoting into a more flexible white-green strategy that seems open."* - Backup Archetype Suggestions : The navigator will track potential secondary archetypes the player could pivot into if their primary archetype fails to come together. For instance, if a player starts drafting an Izzet spells deck but isn’t seeing enough instant or sorcery payoffs, the navigator might suggest pivoting into a slower control-based Izzet deck or splashing for a different color. Prompt Example : - *"You’re not getting enough spells for a traditional Izzet deck—consider pivoting into a control archetype or splashing a third color for additional threats."* ### 3. Splashing Splashing refers to adding a third color to your deck to play a few powerful cards that aren’t in your primary colors. worked in their draft and what could be improved | --- ### Key Features and What We Learn from Archetypist 1. Archetype Identification : - What It Does : Tracks the most common archetypes appearing in successful decks (e.g., 7-0 or 7-1 decks). - What We Learn : This feature helps the strategy module recognize which archetypes are performing best in a given draft format, allowing the player to make more informed decisions about which strategies to pursue. 2. Synergy Optimization : - What It Does : Measures how well cards within a deck synergize with each other. - What We Learn : This helps the strategy module focus on maximizing synergy in deck-building, particularly in archetypes that rely heavily on interaction between specific cards (e.g., tribal decks or combo strategies). 3. Win Rate Tracking : - What It Does : Tracks win rates of specific archetypes over time. - What We Learn : By knowing which archetypes are winning more games, the strategy module can adjust its recommendations and help the player gravitate towards decks with the highest success rate. 4. Card Frequency Analysis : - What It Does : Analyzes how often certain cards show up in successful decks. - What We Learn : Certain cards are essential to archetypes, and by understanding how often these cards appear, the module can prioritize them in drafts as key pieces for specific strategies. 5. Pivot Recommendations : - What It Does : Detects when a player should pivot to another archetype based on card availability and draft signals. - What We Learn : This helps the strategy module guide players to switch archetypes when their current strategy isn’t being supported by the cards being passed, ensuring they can recover and build a viable deck. 6. Archetype Success Over Time : - What It Does : Tracks how the success rate of archetypes changes as the metagame evolves. - What We Learn : The module can provide advice on when certain archetypes are falling out of favor or when a once-overlooked archetype is gaining success, keeping recommendations fresh and meta-aware. 7. Meta Shift Detection : - What It Does : Detects shifts in which archetypes are most successful or falling behind in the meta. - What We Learn : The module can alert players to changing trends, guiding them away from archetypes that are underperforming and toward emerging, successful strategies. 8. Archetype Synergy Grading : - What It Does : Grades how well a player’s deck fits within an archetype’s known synergies. - What We Learn : Real-time feedback during drafting allows players to know whether their deck is coming together well or if it lacks key synergy pieces, helping players refine their picks. 9. Archetype Flexibility : - What It Does : Analyzes how well a card fits into multiple archetypes. - What We Learn : This helps players pick cards that work in multiple archetypes, allowing them to stay open to different draft paths early on and increasing the chances of pivoting smoothly if needed. 10. Post-Draft Analysis : - What It Does : Provides insights into why specific archetypes succeeded or failed based on their construction. - What We Learn : By reflecting on post-draft results, players can better understand their decisions and improve their drafting strategy in future games, guided by the patterns seen in successful trophy decks. --- ### Integrating These Features into Our Strategy Module By leveraging the tools and insights from Archetypist , our strategy module will become a comprehensive guide for players at every stage of the draft. These features will not only make the draft experience smoother and more intuitive, but also teach players to think strategically, optimize their decks for synergy, and stay informed about the evolving metagame. The data-driven approach of both 17lands and Archetypist allows the strategy module to dynamically adapt its suggestions based on real-time meta information, win rates, and archetype trends. With these insights, players will gain access to the most effective drafting tools, ensuring they can build winning decks and evolve as players in the ever-changing world of Magic: The Gathering .

Here, we'll integrate 17lands API for trophy deck data, card win rates, and average last seen at (ALSA) statistics, as well as Archetypist for synergy tracking. // Example of fetching trophy decks and win rates from 17lands API async function fetchTrophyDecks() { try { const response = await fetch('https://www.17lands.com/api/trophy_decks'); const data = await response.json(); return data; } catch (error) { console.error('Error fetching trophy decks:', error); } } // Fetching card win rates from 17lands API async function fetchCardWinRates() { try { const response = await fetch('https://www.17lands.com/api/card_win_rates'); const data = await response.json(); return data; } catch (error) { console.error('Error fetching card win rates:', error); } } // Example usage: fetchTrophyDecks().then(decks => console.log('Trophy Decks:', decks)); fetchCardWinRates().then(rates => console.log('Card Win Rates:', rates)); #### Step 2: Enhancing Draft Feedback with Real-Time Data Once you have trophy data and win rates, the strategy module can use this data to provide real-time feedback during drafts. This will allow the module to assess each pick’s value, recommend synergy picks, and suggest when to pivot or splash based on actual performance data from recent drafts. // Function to provide draft pick suggestions based on card win rate and synergy async function suggestDraftPick(deck, availableCards) { let winRates = await fetchCardWinRates(); availableCards.forEach(card => { let winRateData = winRates.find(rate => rate.cardName === card.name); if (winRateData) { card.winRate = winRateData.winRate; } else { card.winRate = 0; // Default win rate if no data found } }); // Sort cards based on their win rates and synergy with the current deck availableCards.sort((a, b) => (b.winRate + calculateSynergy([b], deck)) - (a.winRate + calculateSynergy([a], deck))); return availableCards[0]; // Recommend the top card based on win rate and synergy } // Example usage let bestPick = suggestDraftPick(currentDeck, availableCards); console.log('Best Pick:', bestPick); #### Step 3: Meta Awareness and Auto-Adjustments The module will use meta trend data from the 17lands API to provide advice on how a player should adjust their draft strategy depending on how archetypes are performing. The system can dynamically suggest archetypes to focus on or avoid based on current trends. // Function to detect meta trends and adjust draft strategy async function adjustDraftStrategy(archetype, currentDeck) { let trophyDecks = await fetchTrophyDecks(); let archetypeDecks = trophyDecks.filter(deck => deck.archetype === archetype); let successRate = archetypeDecks.length / trophyDecks.length; if (successRate > 0.5) { return The ${archetype} archetype is performing well in the current meta. Continue drafting cards that support this strategy. ; } else { return The ${archetype} archetype is underperforming. Consider pivoting if you see strong signals for another color or archetype. ; } } // Example usage adjustDraftStrategy('Red-Black Sacrifice', currentDeck).then(advice => console.log(advice)); #### Step 4: Integrating Archetypist Synergy Data We’ll also integrate Archetypist’s data to enhance the synergy detection. By connecting the draft picks and deck composition to Archetypist’s synergy scores, we can ensure the draft navigator provides accurate feedback on how well the player’s picks are working together. // Fetching synergy data from Archetypist (pseudo-API call) async function fetchSynergyData(deck) { try { const response = await fetch('https://www.archetypist.com/api/synergy', { method: 'POST', headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' }, body: JSON.stringify(deck) }); const synergyData = await response.json(); return synergyData; } catch (error) { console.error('Error fetching synergy data:', error); } } // Example usage fetchSynergyData(currentDeck).then(data => console.log('Synergy Data:', data)); #### Step 5: Gameplay and Post-Game Review Integration Once the draft is complete and the player begins playing games, the gameplay module can provide in-game advice and post-game reviews. ### Completion: Signal Detection, Classification, and Final Features Now that we’ve fleshed out the critical aspects of the draft navigator , we’ll finish by detailing how the app will detect signals during a draft and classify them to inform decision-making. Afterward, I’ll provide a complete summary of the remaining features. ### Step 18: Signal Detection and Classification in Drafting Signal detection is crucial for drafting effectively in Magic: The Gathering. Signals refer to cards passed by other players that indicate which colors or archetypes are open (i.e., under-drafted by other players). The app’s signal detection module will analyze the packs being passed to the player, classify the strength of the signals, and provide recommendations accordingly. #### Key Aspects of Signal Detection : 1. Color Signals : The system will track how many cards of each color (or combination of colors) are being passed across multiple picks. This helps the player determine which colors are most open. 2. Archetype Signals : Beyond just color, the system will track archetype-defining cards that are being passed, signaling if a specific deck archetype (e.g., Red-Black Sacrifice) is likely to be underdrafted. 3. Adjusting to Draft Pack Context : The system will also classify the strength of the cards being passed, taking into account factors like card rarity and power level to give better context on whether a color or archetype is truly open. 4. Cumulative Signal Strength : The app will continuously monitor these signals and combine them into a cumulative signal score that updates over the course of the draft. The player can then see if a color is consistently open and decide if a pivot is warranted. --- #### How Signal Detection Works : The system uses the available draft data to track how often certain colors or archetypes appear in the player’s draft pool and across packs they’ve passed. Each time a new pack is opened, the app logs which colors or archetype-defining cards are present. It then tracks these patterns over several picks to build a signal profile . ##### Step-by-Step Signal Detection Process : 1. Initial Data Logging : - When a new pack is passed to the player, the app logs the color of each card in the pack and whether any key archetype cards are present. 2. Classifying Color Signals : - The app counts how many cards of each color are passed to the player and tracks trends over multiple packs. For example, if Red cards are consistently being passed after the first five picks, it might signal that Red is open. // Function to track and classify color signals function detectColorSignals(passedPacks, deck) { let colorCounts = { red: 0, blue: 0, green: 0, black: 0, white: 0 }; // Count cards of each color in passed packs passedPacks.forEach(pack => { pack.forEach(card => { if (card.color) { colorCounts[card.color]++; } }); }); // Determine if a color is open based on frequency let openColor = Object.keys(colorCounts).reduce((a, b) => colorCounts[a] > colorCounts[b] ? a : b); return Strong signals for ${openColor} detected. Consider drafting in this color. ; } // Example usage: let signalAdvice = detectColorSignals(passedPacks, currentDeck); console.log(signalAdvice); 3. Classifying Archetype Signals : - The system also checks for archetype-defining cards (e.g., sacrifice enablers, lifegain payoffs) in the passed packs. If several key archetype cards are passed in consecutive packs, the system will flag this as a potential archetype to pivot into. // Function to detect archetype signals based on key cards function detectArchetypeSignals(passedPacks, archetypeKeyCards) { let archetypeSignalCount = 0; passedPacks.forEach(pack => { pack.forEach(card => { if (archetypeKeyCards.includes(card.name)) { archetypeSignalCount++; } }); }); return archetypeSignalCount > 3 ? 'Strong signals for archetype detected. Consider pivoting.' : 'No strong archetype signals yet.'; } // Example usage: let archetypeKeyCards = ['Claim the Firstborn', 'Village Rites']; // Red-Black Sacrifice key cards let archetypeAdvice = detectArchetypeSignals(passedPacks, archetypeKeyCards); console.log(archetypeAdvice); 4. Cumulative Signal Score : - The app aggregates signals over the course of the draft to create a cumulative signal score . If a player repeatedly sees cards of a certain color or archetype, the score increases, signaling that it’s safe to pivot. // Function to track cumulative signal score across the draft function cumulativeSignalScore(passedPacks, deck) { let signalStrength = 0; passedPacks.forEach(pack => { pack.forEach(card => { if (deck.colors.includes(card.color)) { signalStrength += 5; // Increment if the passed card matches the player's color } }); }); return signalStrength > 20 ? 'Strong signals to continue drafting this color' : 'Weak signals. Consider pivoting.'; } // Example usage: let cumulativeScore = cumulativeSignalScore(passedPacks, currentDeck); console.log(cumulativeScore); --- ### Step 19: Finishing Key Features and Refining UI We’ll now polish the user interface (UI) to reflect the signal detection and provide clearer feedback to the player. With this, we have a fully functional signal detection module that integrates with the draft feedback UI to help players make smarter decisions based on real-time data. --- ### Can We Tell from 17Lands Data Which Archetypes Are More Likely to Be Open? Yes, 17Lands data can help identify which archetypes are more likely to be open in a draft by analyzing a few key metrics. One of the most useful metrics for determining this is Average Last Seen At (ALSA) data for individual cards. ALSA tells us how late in a draft a card is typically still available when it's picked. Cards with high ALSA values are often undervalued by the player base and therefore more likely to be passed later in the draft. #### How ALSA Helps Detect Open Archetypes : - Higher ALSA values for key cards in an archetype indicate that the archetype is more likely to be open in a given draft. If archetype-defining cards (e.g., "Claim the Firstborn" in Red-Black Sacrifice) consistently have a high ALSA, it means that other players are undervaluing that archetype, allowing you to draft it more easily. - Win Rate vs ALSA : Combining the win rate of a card or archetype with its ALSA can provide powerful insights. If an archetype has a high win rate but its key cards are being seen late in drafts (high ALSA), this indicates that the archetype is underdrafted and therefore likely to be open. #### How to Use ALSA and Win Rate Data to Inform Draft Decisions : 1. Track ALSA of Archetype Cards : The app will monitor the ALSA of key cards for different archetypes. If the ALSA is high, it will suggest that the archetype is underdrafted. 2. Compare ALSA with Win Rate : If an archetype’s key cards are still available late in the draft but have a high win rate, the app will recommend drafting that archetype, as it’s likely to perform well and be less contested by other players. 3. Dynamic Recommendations : During the draft, the app will dynamically track which cards are being passed and compare this to ALSA data. If the user is seeing cards with a higher ALSA than expected, the app will flag that archetype as open and suggest pivoting into it. --- ### Code Snippet for Using ALSA to Detect Open Archetypes : // Function to detect which archetypes are likely open based on ALSA data async function detectOpenArchetypes(deck, availableCards) { let ALSAData = await fetchALSAData(); // Fetch ALSA data from 17Lands API let archetypeSignals = {}; // Track ALSA for archetype-defining cards availableCards.forEach(card => { let cardALSA = ALSAData.find(data => data.cardName === card.name)?.ALSA || 0; let winRate = ALSAData.find(data => data.cardName === card.name)?.winRate || 0; // If a key archetype card is still available late in the draft, flag the archetype as open if (cardALSA > 5 && winRate > 50) { archetypeSignals[card.archetype] = (archetypeSignals[card.archetype] || 0) + 1; } }); // Return archetypes that have strong signals of being open let openArchetypes = Object.keys(archetypeSignals).filter(archetype => archetypeSignals[archetype] > 3); return openArchetypes.length ? Consider drafting ${openArchetypes.join(', ')}. Strong signals detected. : 'No clear open archetypes detected.'; } // Example usage: detectOpenArchetypes(currentDeck, availableCards).then(advice => console.log(advice)); ### How the App Will Use ALSA and Win Rate Data to Improve Strategy : 1. Monitor ALSA Trends : The app will regularly pull ALSA data for key cards across archetypes and track how late those cards are being passed in drafts. 2. Real-Time Alerts : If the player sees a card that has a high ALSA, indicating that the archetype is likely open, the app will flag it in real-time. 3. Synergy and Power Considerations : The app will consider both synergy and raw power when analyzing the ALSA and win rate data, ensuring that players aren’t simply chasing open archetypes but also optimizing for deck strength. 4. Post-Draft Analysis : After the draft, the app will analyze which archetypes were open based on ALSA and whether the player successfully identified those signals during their draft. --- ### Summary: Using ALSA to Detect Open Archetypes By leveraging ALSA and win rate data from 17Lands , the app can effectively help players identify which archetypes are more likely to be open in a draft. The system will track key archetype-defining cards and provide real-time feedback on whether the player should pivot to an underdrafted archetype based on the cards they are seeing. This approach ensures that players: - Maximize their chances of drafting into a successful archetype. - Avoid competing for contested archetypes. - Make informed decisions based on both data-driven signals and strategic insights during the draft. The combination of signal detection, ALSA analysis, and dynamic feedback will create a robust tool for improving draft performance and helping players adapt to changing draft environments and meta shifts .

This weighted formula ensures the recommendation takes into account not just how late a card might be available, but also its overall strength and fit within the deck. ##### Code Implementation for Weighted Draft Picks : // Function to calculate a card's overall pick score based on ALSA, win rate, and synergy function calculatePickScore(card, deck, ALSAData) { let cardALSA = ALSAData.find(data => data.cardName === card.name)?.ALSA || 0; let winRate = ALSAData.find(data => data.cardName === card.name)?.winRate || 0; let synergyScore = calculateSynergy([card], deck); // Weigh ALSA, win rate, and synergy (adjust weights as needed) let ALSAWeight = 0.3; let winRateWeight = 0.4; let synergyWeight = 0.3; let overallScore = (cardALSA * ALSAWeight) + (winRate * winRateWeight) + (synergyScore * synergyWeight); return overallScore; } // Example usage during draft: let availableCards = [...]; // Array of card objects availableCards.forEach(card => { let pickScore = calculatePickScore(card, currentDeck, ALSAData); console.log( Card: ${card.name}, Pick Score: ${pickScore} ); }); #### How This Works in Drafting : - ALSA Weight : Helps the player identify cards that are more likely to be available later in the draft, allowing for floating high-value cards when it’s safe to do so. - Win Rate Weight : Prioritizes high-performing cards that historically win more games, ensuring the player drafts powerful cards. - Synergy Weight : Ensures that the cards drafted are cohesive and work well within the existing strategy or archetype. --- ### Step 21: Pivoting Advice Based on Meta Shifts Beyond individual card picks, one of the most important strategic decisions during a draft is knowing when to pivot . Create a Beta Testing Program : Invite a select group of users (through Discord, email lists, Magic communities) to participate in the beta program. 2. Speed and Performance : Ensure that real-time recommendations and meta updates load quickly, even when pulling large datasets from external APIs. 2. Refining Draft Suggestions : Fine-tune the weighted recommendation system, balancing the use of ALSA, win rates, and synergy to provide more accurate suggestions. 3. UI and UX Improvements : Address any user feedback regarding the app’s interface to make it as intuitive as possible. ##### Example of Optimizing Data Handling for Faster Performance : // Function to optimize API data retrieval by caching meta updates async function getCachedMetaUpdates() { let lastMetaUpdate = localStorage.getItem('lastMetaUpdate'); let now = Date.now(); // If meta data is outdated, fetch new data and update cache if (!lastMetaUpdate || (now - new Date(lastMetaUpdate).getTime()) > 86400000) { // 24 hours let updatedMeta = await pullMetaUpdates(); localStorage.setItem('cachedMetaData', JSON.stringify(updatedMeta)); localStorage.setItem('lastMetaUpdate', new Date().toISOString()); return updatedMeta; } // Return cached meta data if it is still valid return JSON.parse(localStorage.getItem('cachedMetaData')); } // Example usage: getCachedMetaUpdates().then(meta => console.log('Current Meta:', meta)); #### Bug Fixes and Testing : - Bug Tracking : Use a bug-tracking tool to monitor any issues raised by beta testers and fix them accordingly. - Unit Testing : Write unit tests to ensure that key functions (e.g., draft suggestions, synergy detection, pivot advice) work as expected in a variety of scenarios. ##### Example Unit Test for Draft Suggestions : // Unit test to check if draft recommendations prioritize high win-rate cards function testDraftRecommendations() { let availableCards = [ { name: 'Shock', winRate: 60, synergy: 50, ALSA: 3 }, { name: 'Lava Coil', winRate: 75, synergy: 40, ALSA: 2 }, { name: 'Pillar of Flame', winRate: 65, synergy: 55, ALSA: 4 } ]; let recommendations = generateDraftRecommendations(availableCards, currentDeck, ALSAData); let topPick = recommendations[0].card; // Test if the top recommendation is the highest win-rate card (Lava Coil) return topPick === 'Lava Coil' ?
